speaker: PROLOGUE
text: I come no more to make you laugh. Things now

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: That bear a weighty and a serious brow,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Sad, high, and working, full of state and woe,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Such noble scenes as draw the eye to flow,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: We now present. Those that can pity here

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: May, if they think it well, let fall a tear;

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: The subject will deserve it. Such as give

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Their money out of hope they may believe

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: May here find truth too. Those that come to see

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Only a show or two, and so agree

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: The play may pass, if they be still and willing,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: I’ll undertake may see away their shilling

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Richly in two short hours. Only they

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: That come to hear a merry, bawdy play,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: A noise of targets, or to see a fellow

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: In a long motley coat guarded with yellow,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Will be deceived. For, gentle hearers, know

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: To rank our chosen truth with such a show

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: As fool and fight is, besides forfeiting

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Our own brains and the opinion that we bring

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: To make that only true we now intend,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Will leave us never an understanding friend.

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Therefore, for goodness’ sake, and as you are known

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: The first and happiest hearers of the town,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Be sad, as we would make you. Think you see

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: The very persons of our noble story

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: As they were living. Think you see them great,

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: And followed with the general throng and sweat

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: Of thousand friends. Then, in a moment, see

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: How soon this mightiness meets misery.

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: And if you can be merry then, I’ll say

speaker: PROLOGUE
text: A man may weep upon his wedding day.
